one_line_takeaway: EvoJail replaces hand-crafted jailbreak obfuscation (Base64/Rot13-style ciphers, code-embedding tricks) with an LLM-driven multi-objective evolutionary search over reversible encryption-decryption prompt pairs, beating 6 baselines on hypervolume (ASR vs. output perplexity) in 15/18 individual-level scenarios, though it trails CodeAttack specifically on GPT-4.1-nano.
key_finding: On the individual-level comparison (Table 1, 18 scenarios), EvoJail attains the highest HV in 15/18 scenarios. On LLaMA-3.1-8B it exceeds HV=0.700 in multiple settings (e.g., LLaMA8B-G2 = 0.770, its highest reported HV), consistently beating FlipAttack and CodeAttack, which the paper states suffer near-total jailbreak failure (ASR near 0) in several LLaMA scenarios despite occasionally scoring well on GPT (e.g., CodeAttack on GPT-G2 = 0.713, GPT-G3 = 0.687, vs. only 0.042-0.050 on LLaMA-7B-G scenarios). CodeChameleon is described as relatively stable but never optimal. On gpt-4.1-nano, EvoJail is competitive with, and outperforms, CodeAttack in half the GPT scenarios (e.g., EvoJail 0.755 vs CodeAttack 0.713 on GPT-G2; EvoJail 0.589 vs CodeAttack 0.551 on GPT-J1). Cipher scores near 0.000 HV in almost every scenario. At the ensemble level on unseen instances (Table 2), EvoJail leads on all LLaMA scenarios (e.g., LLaMA-7B-G: EvoJail 0.762 vs. next-best Jailbroken/CodeChameleon 0.340) and is competitive-but-second to CodeAttack on the GPT scenarios (GPT-G: CodeAttack 0.688 vs EvoJail 0.676; GPT-J: CodeAttack 0.610 vs EvoJail 0.549). An ensemble-scaling analysis found the number of EvoJail strategies needed to fully dominate a baseline varies by scenario (e.g., >=14 strategies needed to dominate Jailbroken on LLaMA-7B, vs. only 4 to dominate CodeChameleon on LLaMA-8B-J), and that EvoJail remains dominated by CodeAttack on GPT-G even at larger ensemble sizes. Qualitative case studies (two example harmful queries) show EvoJail's decoded outputs produce explicit multi-step 'execution plans' (e.g., social-engineering/phishing steps) whereas FlipAttack, CodeAttack, and ReNeLLM triggered refusals and CodeChameleon/Jailbroken/Cipher produced remedial, semantically-drifted, or garbled content instead of actionable harmful plans.
core_contribution: The paper presents EvoJail, an automated framework that discovers 'long-tail distribution' jailbreak attacks against LLMs via multi-objective evolutionary search, replacing hand-crafted encryption/obfuscation rules used in prior work (e.g., Caesar/Base64/Rot13 ciphers, FlipAttack, CodeChameleon, CodeAttack). Each candidate attack is represented as a tuple x=(h,e,d,t): a natural-language heuristic h describing the obfuscation design principle, concrete Python encryption/decryption function implementations (e,d) that must satisfy D(E(q))=q, and a prompt template t sampled from a fixed template pool. The framework jointly optimizes two objectives -- maximizing attack success rate (ASR) and minimizing output perplexity (PPL, computed with GPT2-XL) of the target model's response -- via a population-based multi-objective evolutionary loop (fast non-dominated sorting, akin to NSGA-II) in which an LLM (DeepSeek-V3) serves as the 'design model' for initialization, mutation, crossover, and a verification-repair loop that iteratively fixes candidate encrypt/decrypt pairs that fail a reversibility check on a reference input.
threat_model: Black-box attacker who can only submit text prompts to a target LLM (LLaMA-2-7B-chat, LLaMA-3.1-8B-Instruct, or gpt-4.1-nano) via its normal API/chat interface and observe outputs; no gradient or logit access is required (the evolutionary search and the DeepSeek-V3 'design model' are attacker-side components separate from the victim model, and PPL is scored with an independent GPT2-XL model, not the victim). The attacker's goal is to obfuscate a harmful query (e.g., self-harm-inducing cyberbullying instructions, evidence-tampering advice) inside a reversible encryption-decryption code pair plus template so the victim model reconstructs and answers the harmful intent while appearing to process a benign/algorithmic task, evading safety alignment trained mostly on natural-language distributions.
limitations: The paper itself notes: (1) EvoJail is dominated by CodeAttack in the GPT-G scenario even as ensemble size increases, attributed to model-architecture/fine-tuning differences rather than a fixable algorithmic gap; (2) ASR is judged by an LLM (DeepSeek-V3) rather than by human or rule-based verification, an automated-judge dependency common to the field but not independently validated in this paper; (3) individuals that fail the reversibility check after Rmax repair attempts may still be retained if merely 'executable,' which the authors acknowledge is a deliberate trade-off between structural rigor and exploratory flexibility, i.e., some retained encrypt/decrypt pairs are not guaranteed to exactly recover the original query; (4) results are reported only on 3 victim models and 18 curated instance combinations, so generalization to other (especially larger, more recent, or heavily safety-tuned) model families is untested; (5) DeepSeek-V3 is used both as the evolutionary 'design model' that authors the attacks and as the ASR judge, a shared-model dependency the paper does not discuss as a potential source of bias. No mitigation/defense evaluation is presented -- the paper's own future-work section defers 'adaptive defenses against long-tail attacks' to subsequent research.

datasets_used: GPTFuzzer harmful-query benchmark (hate speech, self-harm, malware generation categories)
JBB-Behaviors (aggregates Trojan Detection Challenge, HarmBench, and AdvBench)
quantitative_results: Table 1 individual-level HV: EvoJail best in 15/18 model-scenario combinations
LLaMA8B-G2 HV: EvoJail 0.770 vs CodeChameleon 0.500, CodeAttack 0.595, Jailbroken 0.500, ReNeLLM 0.525, FlipAttack 0.372, Cipher 0.020
GPT-G2 HV: CodeAttack 0.713 vs EvoJail 0.755 (EvoJail higher)
LLaMA7B-G2 HV: CodeAttack 0.042 vs EvoJail 0.670 (CodeAttack near-total failure on this model)
Table 2 ensemble-level HV on unseen instances: LLaMA-7B-G EvoJail 0.762 vs next-best 0.340 (CodeChameleon/Jailbroken tie)
Table 2 GPT-G ensemble HV: CodeAttack 0.688 vs EvoJail 0.676 (CodeAttack higher)
Table 2 GPT-J ensemble HV: CodeAttack 0.610 vs EvoJail 0.549 (CodeAttack higher)
Ensemble-scaling: >=14 EvoJail strategies needed to dominate Jailbroken on LLaMA-7B; only 4 needed to dominate CodeChameleon on LLaMA-8B-J
EvoJail configuration: 20 iterations, population size N=10, Rmax=10 repair attempts, seeded from 5 ancestor strategies (Queue, OddEven, Length, Stack, Reverse)
